pairs.question: How much upfront cash should a first‑time buyer prepare before purchasing a house in Malaysia in 2026?
pairs.answer: The guide advises preparing between 15% and 18% of the property price as upfront cash.
pairs.title: The Process of Buying a House in Malaysia 2026

pairs.question: What is the typical amount of the earnest deposit when making an offer on a property?
pairs.answer: The earnest deposit is usually 2% of the purchase price.
pairs.title: The Process of Buying a House in Malaysia 2026

pairs.question: What are the key steps a buyer must follow after making an offer, according to the 2026 buying guide?
pairs.answer: After making an offer, the buyer should (1) apply for a housing loan (Bank Letter of Offer and Financing Agreement), (2) sign the Sales & Purchase Agreement (SPA), (3) sign the financing documents such as the Financing Agreement, Deed of Assignment/Power of Attorney or Memorandum of Completion, (4) pay stamp duty and other legal fees, and (5) complete the final handover and sign the Memorandum of Transfer (MOT).
pairs.title: The Process of Buying a House in Malaysia 2026

pairs.question: What is the title of the guide mentioned in the document?
pairs.answer: The guide is titled "Step-by-Step Guide to Buying a House in Malaysia (2026)".
pairs.title: Step-by-Step Guide to Buying a House in Malaysia

pairs.question: In which year was the guide published?
pairs.answer: The guide was published in 2026, as indicated by the title.
pairs.title: Step-by-Step Guide to Buying a House in Malaysia

pairs.question: Which organization is associated with the guide?
pairs.answer: The guide is associated with IQI, as shown by the "Join IQI" prompt in the document.
pairs.title: Step-by-Step Guide to Buying a House in Malaysia

pairs.question: What is the title of the guide?
pairs.answer: The title is "Complete Guide to Purchasing Property in Malaysia".
pairs.title: Complete Guide to Purchasing Property in Malaysia

pairs.question: In which year was this guide released?
pairs.answer: The guide was released in 2026.
pairs.title: Complete Guide to Purchasing Property in Malaysia

pairs.question: Which organization is associated with this guide?
pairs.answer: The guide is associated with IQI, as indicated by the phrase "Join IQI" in the document.
pairs.title: Complete Guide to Purchasing Property in Malaysia

pairs.question: What is the title of the guide?
pairs.answer: Complete Guide For First Time Home Buyer to Buying a House in Malaysia
pairs.title: Complete Guide For First Time Home Buyer

pairs.question: When was the guide published?
pairs.answer: 26 February 2026
pairs.title: Complete Guide For First Time Home Buyer

pairs.question: Which company provides this guide?
pairs.answer: StashAway Malaysia
pairs.title: Complete Guide For First Time Home Buyer

pairs.question: What is property stamp duty according to the guide?
pairs.answer: Property stamp duty is a tax charged on legal instruments, not directly on the property.
pairs.title: Property Stamp Duty in Malaysia: How to Calculate

pairs.question: Which legal documents does the guide say stamp duty is calculated on when buying a home?
pairs.answer: Stamp duty is calculated on the Sale and Purchase Agreement (SPA), the Memorandum of Transfer (MOT) or Deed of Assignment (DOA), and the loan agreement.
pairs.title: Property Stamp Duty in Malaysia: How to Calculate

pairs.question: Besides stamp duty, what other cost does the guide advise first‑time buyers to budget for?
pairs.answer: The guide advises budgeting for legal fees in addition to stamp duty.
pairs.title: Property Stamp Duty in Malaysia: How to Calculate

pairs.question: What is the total MOT stamp duty a Malaysian citizen would pay on a RM 750,000 property?
pairs.answer: The stamp duty is calculated on each tranche: 1% on the first RM 100,000 (RM1,000), 2% on the next RM 400,000 (RM8,000), and 3% on the remaining RM 250,000 (RM7,500). The total MOT stamp duty equals RM1,000 + RM8,000 + RM7,500 = RM16,500.
pairs.title: Stamp Duty (MOT) Malaysia 2026

pairs.question: How much stamp duty must a foreign buyer pay on a RM 1,200,000 property?
pairs.answer: Foreign buyers are charged a flat 8% rate on the full property value, so the stamp duty is 8% of RM1,200,000 = RM96,000.
pairs.title: Stamp Duty (MOT) Malaysia 2026

pairs.question: What stamp duty exemption is available for first‑time citizen buyers and up to what property price does it apply?
pairs.answer: First‑time citizen buyers receive a 100% exemption on MOT stamp duty for properties priced up to RM500,000, effective until 31 December 2027.
pairs.title: Stamp Duty (MOT) Malaysia 2026

pairs.question: What stamp duty exemption does a first‑time buyer receive on the Memorandum of Transfer (MOT) for a property priced up to RM500,000?
pairs.answer: First‑time buyers purchasing properties up to RM500,000 are eligible for a stamp duty exemption on the MOT, resulting in a MOT stamp duty of RM0.00.
pairs.title: Stamp Duty Calculator Malaysia 2026

pairs.question: How are the legal fees for the MOT calculated under the Solicitors' Remuneration Order?
pairs.answer: Legal fees for the MOT are calculated on a tiered scale based on the property value: 1.00% of the value for the first RM500,000 (minimum RM500). In the example of a RM500,000 residential property, the MOT legal fee is RM6,250.00.
pairs.title: Stamp Duty Calculator Malaysia 2026

pairs.question: What is the estimated total upfront cost (stamp duty plus legal fees) for a RM500,000 residential property bought by a Malaysian citizen who is a first‑time buyer?
pairs.answer: The estimated total upfront cost is RM11,875.00, comprising MOT stamp duty RM0.00, MOT legal fees RM6,250.00, loan stamp duty RM0.00, and loan legal fees RM5,625.00.
pairs.title: Stamp Duty Calculator Malaysia 2026

pairs.question: How is the MOT stamp duty calculated for a residential property purchase of RM750,000?
pairs.answer: The MOT duty is tiered: 1% on the first RM100,000 (RM1,000), 2% on the next RM400,000 (RM8,000), and 3% on the remaining RM250,000 (RM7,500). Total MOT duty = RM1,000 + RM8,000 + RM7,500 = RM16,500.
pairs.title: Malaysian Stamp Duty Calculator

pairs.question: What are the eligibility criteria for the first‑time buyer stamp‑duty exemption?
pairs.answer: You must be a Malaysian citizen buying your first residential property in your own name, and the purchase price must be ≤ RM1 million (with a 100 % exemption if the price is ≤ RM500,000).
pairs.title: Malaysian Stamp Duty Calculator

pairs.question: What stamp duty is payable on a loan agreement for a mortgage of RM800,000?
pairs.answer: The loan‑agreement duty is 0.5 % of the loan principal, so RM800,000 × 0.5 % = RM4,000.
pairs.title: Malaysian Stamp Duty Calculator

pairs.question: What is the typical Debt Service Ratio (DSR) ceiling range that Malaysian banks use to approve a home loan?
pairs.answer: Most Malaysian banks approve a home loan if your total monthly debt commitments stay within roughly 60-70% of net income.
pairs.title: How Much Home Loan Can You Afford? DSR Explained

pairs.question: How does an existing car loan impact the amount of home loan you can afford?
pairs.answer: A car loan eats a huge chunk of your DSR, reducing the portion of income available for the new home loan instalment and thus lowering the loan amount you can qualify for.
pairs.title: How Much Home Loan Can You Afford? DSR Explained

pairs.question: According to the guide, what net monthly income supports a home loan of about RM350,000 to RM450,000 at current rates?
pairs.answer: A net monthly income of roughly RM5,000 can support a loan of around RM350,000‑450,000 at current rates.
pairs.title: How Much Home Loan Can You Afford? DSR Explained

pairs.question: What does DSR stand for and how is it calculated?
pairs.answer: DSR stands for Debt Service Ratio. It measures the percentage of your net monthly income that goes toward servicing all debt obligations. It is calculated as (Total Monthly Debt Commitments ÷ Net Monthly Income) × 100%.
pairs.title: DSR Calculation Malaysia: Home Loan Eligibility

pairs.question: What DSR thresholds do most Malaysian banks use, and are there any higher limits for certain borrowers?
pairs.answer: Most Malaysian banks set a DSR threshold of 60% to 70%. Some banks may allow up to 80% for high‑income borrowers, typically those with a net monthly income above RM 10,000.
pairs.title: DSR Calculation Malaysia: Home Loan Eligibility

pairs.question: Which types of financial commitments are counted as debt when calculating DSR?
pairs.answer: Banks count all recurring financial commitments, including: existing home financing (actual monthly instalment), car loan/hire purchase (actual monthly instalment), personal loan (actual monthly instalment), PTPTN repayment (even if in deferment), credit card (5% of the approved credit limit, not the outstanding balance), and ASB financing (actual monthly instalment).
pairs.title: DSR Calculation Malaysia: Home Loan Eligibility

pairs.question: What is the maximum loan‑to‑value (LTV) ratio for a first or second housing loan in Malaysia, and what down‑payment percentage does that correspond to?
pairs.answer: BNM allows up to a 90% LTV for the 1st and 2nd housing loans, which means the buyer must provide a 10% down‑payment from their own funds.
pairs.title: How Much Loan Can You Get? LTV Rules 2026

pairs.question: How much cash does a Malaysian buyer need upfront for a RM500,000 property if they obtain a 70% loan margin, including stamp duty, MOT, legal and valuation fees?
pairs.answer: At a 70% loan margin the buyer needs about RM170,000 cash upfront (30% down‑payment plus the additional costs that bring the total to roughly 34% of the property value).
pairs.title: How Much Loan Can You Get? LTV Rules 2026

pairs.question: What LTV limit applies to foreign buyers and how does their stamp duty differ from that of Malaysian buyers?
pairs.answer: Foreign buyers are capped at a typical LTV of 60‑70% regardless of the number of properties, and they pay a higher stamp duty of 8% (versus the tiered 1‑4% rate for Malaysians), which is already included in the higher upfront cash requirement.
pairs.title: How Much Loan Can You Get? LTV Rules 2026

pairs.question: What is the interest rate and monthly repayment for the Maybank Maxi Home loan?
pairs.answer: The Maybank Maxi Home loan has an interest rate from 4.35% per annum and a monthly repayment of RM2,810.62 (tenure over 20 years).
pairs.title: Best Housing Loans in Malaysia 2026

pairs.question: Which housing loan products have the lowest interest rate, and what is the monthly repayment for those loans?
pairs.answer: The lowest interest rate is 2.75% per annum, offered by MBSB Property Refinancing-i, Hong Leong Housing Guarantee Scheme, and Hong Leong Refinancing Property Loan. Each of these loans has a monthly repayment of RM2,439.75 (tenure over 20 years).
pairs.title: Best Housing Loans in Malaysia 2026

pairs.question: What is the profit rate and monthly repayment for Bank Islam Baiti Home Financing-i?
pairs.answer: Bank Islam Baiti Home Financing-i has a profit rate from 3.8% per annum and a monthly repayment of RM2,679.72 (tenure over 20 years).
pairs.title: Best Housing Loans in Malaysia 2026

pairs.question: What is the formula used to calculate the Debt‑to‑Service Ratio (DSR)?
pairs.answer: DSR (%) = (Total Monthly Commitments ÷ Net Monthly Income) × 100%.
pairs.title: DSR Calculator

pairs.question: Which components are included in the Net Monthly Income for DSR calculation?
pairs.answer: Net Monthly Income is your take‑home pay after EPF, SOCSO and income tax deductions, and includes your basic salary plus any fixed regular allowances such as transport or housing allowances. One‑off bonuses, overtime pay or irregular income are not included unless they can be documented reliably over 6‑12 months.
pairs.title: DSR Calculator

pairs.question: What types of debt payments are counted as Total Monthly Commitments in the DSR calculation?
pairs.answer: Total Monthly Commitments include every debt payment reported to CCRIS or CTOS, such as existing home loan, car loan, personal loan, PTPTN education loan and the minimum payment on credit cards. Regular expenses like rent, utilities or groceries are not included.
pairs.title: DSR Calculator

pairs.question: What upfront costs does the calculator estimate I need to pay when purchasing a home?
pairs.answer: The estimated upfront costs are a 10% down payment of RM 72,366, stamp duty of about RM 15,710, legal fees of about RM 6,789, totaling roughly RM 94,865.
pairs.title: Home Loan Eligibility Calculator 2026

pairs.question: How much home loan and property price can I qualify for if I am buying my first property?
pairs.answer: For a first‑property purchase you can qualify for up to RM 651,292 in loan amount, which allows a maximum property price of about RM 723,658 at 90 % financing.
pairs.title: Home Loan Eligibility Calculator 2026

pairs.question: What Debt Service Ratio (DSR) limits and income figures are used in the example to determine loan eligibility?
pairs.answer: The example uses a monthly gross income of RM 8,000, existing commitments of RM 1,500, a maximum 60 % DSR allowance of RM 4,800, leaving RM 3,300 available for loan repayments. The current DSR is 18.8 % and would rise to 60 % after taking the loan.
pairs.title: Home Loan Eligibility Calculator 2026

pairs.question: What type of property insurance is mandatory for a Malaysian home loan and why?
pairs.answer: Fire insurance (or fire takaful) is the only mandatory insurance because the bank requires it to protect its collateral; if the building burns down the bank would lose its security while the loan remains outstanding.
pairs.title: Property Insurance Malaysia: Fire, MRTA & Homeowner Coverage Compared

pairs.question: How much does a typical fire insurance premium cost per year for a RM500,000 property?
pairs.answer: The typical annual premium for fire insurance on a RM500,000 property is between RM200 and RM400.
pairs.title: Property Insurance Malaysia: Fire, MRTA & Homeowner Coverage Compared

pairs.question: What is the typical cost range for a single‑premium MRTT policy for a RM500,000 property?
pairs.answer: A single‑premium MRTT (Mortgage Reducing Term Term) policy for a RM500,000 property usually costs between RM10,000 and RM30,000.
pairs.title: Property Insurance Malaysia: Fire, MRTA & Homeowner Coverage Compared

pairs.question: What type of insurance does a Malaysian bank require before it releases the loan for a mortgaged property?
pairs.answer: The bank requires fire insurance on the financed property before drawdown, with specific requirements such as the right insured, sum insured basis, perils covered, the bank named as loss payee under an assignment or banker's clause, and a policy period covering the drawdown date forward.
pairs.title: Bank Loan Fire Insurance Malaysia: Requirements

pairs.question: Who must be named as the insured on the fire insurance policy for a bank‑financed property?
pairs.answer: The borrower (the property owner) must be the named insured; policies issued in a different entity name, such as a related company, will be rejected.
pairs.title: Bank Loan Fire Insurance Malaysia: Requirements

pairs.question: What sum insured basis does the bank expect for the fire insurance policy?
pairs.answer: The bank expects the sum insured to be based on the building's reinstatement value, not the market value or the purchase price, which may be lower than the reinstatement cost.
pairs.title: Bank Loan Fire Insurance Malaysia: Requirements
